title: Visualizing in Many Dimensions
concept: While simple linear regression finds a 2D line of best fit, multiple regression builds high-dimensional planes to cut through multi-variable space.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between simple linear regression and multiple linear regression?

options.text: Simple regression uses one input feature, while multiple regression uses two or more input features.
Simple regression is for numerical outputs, while multiple regression is only for categories.
Simple regression uses gradient descent, while multiple regression only uses random guessing.

exercises.explain: Correct! Multiple linear regression extends simple linear regression by using multiple input features to predict a single target.

cards.title: Escaping flatland
cards.body: We live in a three-dimensional world, but most charts show only two axes: X and Y. When you predict a value using multiple inputs, your data breaks free from flat 2D pages.

cards.title: Planes and Hyperplanes
cards.body: In multiple regression, we don't draw a line. With two inputs, the model fits a flat 2D sheet called a plane. With three or more inputs, it creates a high-dimensional hyperplane.

cards.title: Slicing through space
cards.body: Imagine a room filled with floating balloons (data points). Simple regression is like trying to pierce them with a stiff wire (a line). Multiple regression is like slicing through them with a flat piece of cardboard (a plane).

cards.title: Mapping the mountains
cards.body: If you predict mountain temperatures using altitude (input 1) and latitude (input 2), your model draws a sloped plane hovering over a 3D terrain grid.

cards.title: You don't need to see it
cards.body: You might think a model is useless if we can't draw its 10-dimensional hyperplane on a screen. Mathematically, the equations work exactly the same way, whether we can visualize them or not.

left.text:
Draws a line of best fit
Fits on a standard flat plot
Easy to see on paper

right.text:
Draws a plane or hyperplane
Needs 3D plots or math formulas
Hard or impossible to draw

exercises.prompt: In a multiple regression model with exactly two input features, what shape represents the model's predictions?

options.text: A flat 2D plane in a 3D space
A straight 1D line on a 2D graph
A single 0D point in empty space

exercises.explain: Correct! With two inputs and one output, the model maps predictions onto a 3D space using a flat plane.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A hyperplane is a flat boundary or surface that exists in spaces with more than three dimensions.
exercises.explain: True. We call flat decision or regression boundaries in four or more dimensions hyperplanes.

exercises.sentence: While simple linear regression finds the best-fitting line, regression with two inputs finds the best-fitting ___.
exercises.explain: With two inputs and one output, the prediction surface is a flat plane.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios describes a regression model that draws a hyperplane?

options.text: Predicting test scores using hours slept, hours studied, and practice tests taken.
Predicting shoe size using only a person's height.
Categorizing emails as spam or inbox based on key phrases.

exercises.explain: Correct! A regression model with three inputs (hours slept, hours studied, practice tests) operates in 4D space, so it uses a hyperplane.

exercises.prompt: Your manager says your team can't use a multiple regression model with five inputs because it is impossible to visualize a 5-dimensional hyperplane for the clients. How do you respond?

options.text: Explain that the math behind hyperplanes works identically in any dimension, and we can present the model's coefficients instead of a 5D plot.
Agree with them and discard four of the inputs so that you can easily draw a simple 2D line graph.
Tell them that we can project the client's screen into five dimensions using special VR headsets.

exercises.explain: Absolutely correct! High-dimensional models are mathematically sound, even if we cannot visually render them. We can explain their behavior using coefficients or predictions rather than plots. Fantastic job mastering multi-dimensional visualization!
